message: chore(beads): close 8kjnm (resolved in 9bfebeb1); lobrq filed as follow-up

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.1","title":"[FCP3/P6.5] Freeze the CLI truth scenario matrix across offline, local, mesh, and degraded modes","description":"Background: CLI truthfulness needs a stable scenario matrix before artifact capture starts. Otherwise different agents will test overlapping fragments and still miss entire knowledge modes.

Scope: define the acceptance matrix for offline artifact inspection, node-local control, mesh-backed truth, degraded operation, and truthful refusal cases. Pin the representative commands, expected provenance states, and required artifacts for each scenario.

Scope: extend the frozen operator-truth matrix in crates/fwc/testdata/operator_truth/fixture_matrix.json and matching cual_integration coverage so the shared acceptance surface explicitly covers the fifth required class, a truthful refusal/denial case, alongside offline, node-local, mesh-backed, and degraded/fallback cases. I am also checking whether the matrix can cheaply freeze minimal artifact/rerun metadata without widening into runtime implementation work.","created_at":"2026-04-05T23:49:28Z"},{"id":2470,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.1","author":"PlumEagle","text":"Claiming a bounded slice under 8jc8f.1: ensure host-preflight denial paths emit explicit denied availability envelopes and align the operator-truth refusal fixture with that contract. Mail transport is currently flapping, so I am keeping the audit trail in beads while continuing implementation.","created_at":"2026-04-06T00:02:17Z"},{"id":2473,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.1","author":"PlumEagle","text":"Bounded scenario-matrix slice landed locally. crates/fwc/testdata/operator_truth/fixture_matrix.json now freezes the missing truthful refusal case () alongside the existing offline, node-local, mesh-backed, degraded, and fallback-derived rows, and every operator-truth row now carries minimal replay metadata (, , ). Matching cual_integration coverage was updated so the matrix accepts refusal-specific fields without forcing a fake availability/provenance envelope, and the invoke denial regression now validates against the shared fixture contract. docs/FWC_Host_First_Truthfulness_Playbook.md plus crates/fwc/testdata/catalog.json were updated to describe the six-class matrix spanning the five required operator-truth modes. Validation so far: jq empty and git diff --check passed locally; rustfmt --edition 2024 --check passes for crates/fwc/tests/cual_integration.rs. The code and docs changes are unchanged; only the shell quoting in the earlier comment was wrong.","created_at":"2026-04-06T00:08:02Z"},{"id":2477,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.1","author":"jemanuel","text":"Verification status for the bounded scenario-matrix slice: local static validation passed (`jq empty` on the updated operator-truth fixtures/catalog, `git diff --check`, and `rustfmt --edition 2024 --check` on crates/fwc/tests/cual_integration.rs). Offloaded verification hit environment drift instead of a repo-local failure: `rch exec -- cargo fmt --check` reported pre-existing unrelated formatting drift in crates/fwc/src/intent.rs; `rch exec -- cargo test --test cual_integration invoke_denial_records_history_and_suggests_recovery_actions -- --nocapture` failed on worker vmi1156319 because the remote cargo registry copy of crate cc 1.2.57 is corrupted/missing source files; and earlier `rch exec -- cargo check -p fwc --tests` failed on worker vmi1227854 inside the external asupersync path dependency (/data/projects/asupersync/src/net/udp.rs, unexpected closing delimiter). I am leaving the slice scoped to the operator-truth fixture/test/docs changes and not widening into unrelated worker/runtime repair in this bead.","created_at":"2026-04-06T00:12:26Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.2","title":"[FCP3/P6.5] Capture offline and node-local CLI truth scenarios with reproducible artifacts","description":"Background: offline and node-local flows are the easiest places for truth semantics to drift because they often look similar to mesh-backed flows from the CLI surface.

Scope: implement and capture the offline and node-local portions of the CLI acceptance matrix. Produce transcripts, snapshots, and structured logs that show exactly what the CLI knows, what it does not know, and how provenance is presented when the answer is not mesh-backed.

Acceptance: offline and node-local truth scenarios are reproducible, artifact-backed, and explicit enough that future regressions in provenance labeling are obvious.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:54:59.724139Z","created_by":"SunnyOak","updated_at":"2026-04-06T04:40:14.766532Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T04:40:14.766382Z","close_reason":"Verified: 4 integration tests pass (offline show + node-local status, both contract and bundle manifest). Scope is intentionally narrow: expose the observability helpers to integration tests, then add replay-bundle assertions for offline_show_workspace_manifest and node_local_status_host_admin using the same frozen availability/provenance vocabulary already captured by the operator-truth matrix.","created_at":"2026-04-06T01:17:08Z"},{"id":2486,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.2","author":"jemanuel","text":"Bounded replay-bundle slice landed locally. `crates/fwc/src/lib.rs` now re-exports `test_observability` so integration tests can reuse the shared bundle contract, and `crates/fwc/tests/cual_integration.rs` now includes helper assertions plus two new acceptance tests that project the canonical offline and node-local truth scenarios into replayable bundle manifests. The new tests assert that the generated manifest preserves the frozen availability/provenance markers, expected phase (`offline-artifact` vs host discovery), and required artifact names from the operator-truth matrix. Local validation is clean (`rustfmt --check` on touched files and `git diff --check`). Offloaded verification is still operationally blocked: both `rch exec -- cargo test ... replayable_bundle_manifest` invocations stayed in 188-root sync planning for the full workspace rather than reaching cargo quickly, so there is no trustworthy remote pass/fail result yet.","created_at":"2026-04-06T01:22:28Z"},{"id":2502,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.2","author":"jemanuel","text":"SunnyMoose 2026-04-06: Verified prior work via rch.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8jc8f.3","title":"[FCP3/P6.5] Capture mesh-backed, degraded, and refusal CLI truth scenarios","description":"Background: mesh-backed and degraded scenarios are where the new architecture either proves itself or overclaims. They need their own artifact pack rather than being treated as an extension of local-only testing.

Scope: implement and capture the mesh-backed, degraded, and truthful-refusal portions of the CLI acceptance matrix. Show not just successful mesh-backed answers but also degraded visibility, partial evidence, and cases where the system must explicitly refuse to overclaim.

Acceptance: mesh-backed truth semantics are exercised under both healthy and unhealthy conditions, and the resulting artifacts are strong enough for later cutover proof and documentation.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T17:55:00.330586Z","created_by":"SunnyOak","updated_at":"2026-04-06T05:29:38.444058Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T05:29:38.443854Z","close_reason":"Verified: 5 integration tests pass (mesh-backed, degraded, refusal). -{"id":"flywheel_connectors-8kjnm","title":"[REVIEW][HIGH] fcp-conformance tests broken: missing battery_defer_threshold_percent + SessionGoldenVector::verify gone","description":"crates/fcp-conformance/tests/fcps_durability_boundary.rs:114 and crates/fcp-conformance/tests/serialization_roundtrip_conformance.rs:134+261 — compile failures on main: (1) RepairControllerConfig { ... } literal is missing a new required field `battery_defer_threshold_percent` (E0063). (2) SessionGoldenVector::verify no longer exists (E0599). Test binaries fcps_durability_boundary and serialization_roundtrip_conformance fail to compile. Repro: CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-swarm-p2 cargo check --offline -p fcp-conformance --tests. Suggested fix: either add `battery_defer_threshold_percent` to the struct literal or provide a Default-based constructor; rename `SessionGoldenVector::verify` call to the current method name or bring back the removed verify associated fn. Reviewer: cc_1.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:11:56.342727Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T18:29:28.937260Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Repro: CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-swarm-p2 cargo check --offline -p fcp-conformance --tests. Suggested fix: either add `battery_defer_threshold_percent` to the struct literal or provide a Default-based constructor; rename `SessionGoldenVector::verify` call to the current method name or bring back the removed verify associated fn. Reviewer: cc_1.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:11:56.342727Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:16:13.936843Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:16:13.936346Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2824,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8kjnm","author":"jemanuel","text":"Both compile failures resolved in commit 9bfebeb1:
- battery_defer_threshold_percent added to RepairControllerConfig literal (fcps_durability_boundary.rs:120)
- SessionGoldenVector::verify un-cfg-gated (vectors/session.rs:178)

Verified via: env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-swarm-p2 cargo check -p fcp-conformance --tests → clean (only pre-existing warnings).

Runtime: fcps_durability_boundary passes clean; serialization_roundtrip_conformance now compiles and 8 of 10 tests pass. The 2 failing tests are capability-token golden-vector drift unmasked by this fix (principal_zone CWT claim added in c828a3e7 without vector regen) — filed separately as flywheel_connectors-lobrq (P1 bug). Out of 8kjnm scope.

— cod_4 / SunnyMoose","created_at":"2026-04-23T19:16:13Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm","title":"Epic: Typed AuthClaims schema shared between fcp-crypto and fcp-core (remove convention coupling)","description":"# Epic: Typed auth-claim schema shared between fcp-crypto and fcp-core

## One-line statement
`fcp-crypto` builds CWT/COSE capability-token claims as raw CBOR labels
and strings; `fcp-core` separately interprets those labels during
verification. The two crates can silently drift while still compiling.
Extract the claim schema into a typed shared surface so the compiler
forces both crates to evolve together.

## Why this matters

FCP's architecture has a clean layering intent:
- `fcp-crypto` owns **cryptographic primitives** — Ed25519, X25519, HPKE,
  COSE framing, Blake3.
- `fcp-core` owns **security semantics** — zones, capabilities, principals,
  provenance, the rules about what a valid claim set means.

Today that layering is violated. `fcp-crypto` has domain knowledge about
what a capability token contains — it literally knows the string names of
FCP-specific claim fields (`capability_id`, `zone_id`, `operations`,
`principal_id`, `instance_id`, `constraints`, etc.) and how they're
encoded on the wire. Every time `fcp-core::CapabilityVerifier` reads one
of those claims during verification, it's trusting that the builder in
`fcp-crypto::cose::CapabilityTokenBuilder` used the exact same string and
the exact same CBOR shape. That contract is maintained by convention
alone.

## The concrete evidence

1. **Builder side** (`crates/fcp-crypto/src/cose.rs:164-198, 256-276`):
   `CapabilityTokenBuilder` methods like `.capability_id(&str)`,
   `.operations(&[&str])`, `.principal_id(&str)`, `.target_instance(&str)`
   insert raw `ciborium::Value::Text(...)` under raw integer labels from
   the `fcp2_claims` module.

2. **Verifier side** (`crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs:1576-1625`):
   `verify_claims_inner` reads those same labels back out, extracts the
   text, and interprets. There's nothing shared between the two crates
   other than the label-integer constants in `fcp2_claims` and
   convention.

3. **Legacy fallback smoking gun**: verifier at `capability.rs:1597-1621`
   contains a "fallback to OPERATIONS if GRANTS absent" branch with the
   comment:

   > "The builder uses fcp2_claims::OPERATIONS for string list."

   Translation: at some point the builder emitted `OPERATIONS` and the
   verifier was updated to accept either shape. That's a schema-drift
   artifact — the exact symptom this epic prevents. With a typed shared
   schema, the compiler would have forced a single canonical encoding.

4. **Type-level-verification advertised** (`capability.rs:919-930`): the
   module docs claim compile-time verification guarantees. Those
   guarantees stop at the claim-schema boundary.

## The cost

- **Silent drift.** Adding a new optional claim field on the builder side
  can be shipped without the verifier ever learning about it — or,
  worse, with the verifier silently ignoring it and breaking whatever
  invariant that claim was meant to enforce.
- **Security contract becomes a reading exercise.** To audit capability
  tokens today, you must cross-reference `cose.rs` builder methods with
  `capability.rs` verifier branches and check every string label matches.
  That's fragile.
- **Hard to version.** There's nowhere to put a `schema_version` field
  whose meaning is understood by both crates.
- **Hard to specify.** There's no canonical "this is what a capability
  token looks like" artifact we can hand to a security reviewer.

## Epic scope

Move claim construction and interpretation behind a single typed schema
owned by `fcp-core`. `fcp-crypto` accepts a typed claims value, emits its
canonical CBOR, and signs the bytes. It stops knowing FCP-level field
names. `fcp-core` re-parses the canonical CBOR back into the typed
structure for verification.

## Architectural decision space

Two reasonable shapes:

**(A) Shared schema module in fcp-core.**  New `fcp_core::capability::
claims::AuthClaims` struct with a canonical CBOR serde impl. `fcp-crypto`
depends on `fcp-core` (it already does for some types) and accepts
`&AuthClaims` in `CapabilityTokenBuilder::build`.

**(B) Separate `fcp-auth-schema` crate.**  New no-deps crate that both
`fcp-crypto` and `fcp-core` depend on. Cleaner dependency graph at the
cost of one more crate.

The ADR (`8n0rm.2`) picks between (A) and (B); the default preference
is (A) unless we find fcp-crypto-depends-on-fcp-core creates a cycle.

## Children (decomposition)

- `8n0rm.1` — Exhaustive claim-schema inventory
- `8n0rm.2` — ADR: typed claims architecture (A vs B)
- `8n0rm.3` — Implement `AuthClaims` + canonical-CBOR serde
- `8n0rm.4` — Refactor `fcp-crypto::cose` to consume `AuthClaims`
- `8n0rm.5` — `schema_version` field + versioning policy
- `8n0rm.6` — Remove `GRANTS`/`OPERATIONS` legacy fallback in verifier
- `8n0rm.7` — Conformance test: byte-equality between builder output and verifier expectation

## Ordering

- 8n0rm.1 gates 8n0rm.2 (design needs the inventory)
- 8n0rm.2 gates 8n0rm.3 (implement the design)
- 8n0rm.3 gates 8n0rm.4 (crypto refactor consumes the type)
- 8n0rm.3 gates 8n0rm.5 (version field lives on AuthClaims)
- 8n0rm.4 gates 8n0rm.6 (legacy fallback is safe to remove only after
  canonical shape is the only shape produced)
- 8n0rm.4 gates 8n0rm.7 (conformance test depends on refactored
  builder)

## What "done" looks like at epic level

- `AuthClaims` is the single source of truth for capability-token claim
  content. Defined in `fcp-core` (or `fcp-auth-schema`) with a round-trip
  serde proof.
- `fcp-crypto` no longer references FCP-specific claim label names; it
  accepts `&AuthClaims` and produces COSE-signed canonical CBOR.
- `fcp-core::CapabilityVerifier` parses into `AuthClaims` and operates
  on the typed fields, not raw CBOR lookups.
- The GRANTS→OPERATIONS legacy-fallback branch is gone.
- There is a cross-crate conformance test asserting byte-for-byte
  round-trip between canonical inputs.
- Schema version field exists with a documented versioning policy.

## Pitfalls to anticipate

- **Circular deps.** If `fcp-crypto` currently depends on `fcp-core`,
  option (A) is free. If not, we must either flip direction, carve out
  option (B), or accept fcp-crypto→fcp-core dependency.
- **Breaking wire compat.** This change MUST be byte-for-byte identical
  to the current canonical CBOR output for existing tokens. The refactor
  is zero-semantic — it only moves code around. Verify via conformance
  tests against existing golden vectors.
- **The CBOR-label constants in `fcp2_claims`.** Those stay put as the
  wire-format contract. `AuthClaims::serde` uses them; direct callers
  should not.
- **Legacy-fallback removal** (8n0rm.6) is a behavior change. If any
  existing token in the wild uses the `OPERATIONS` shape, removing the
  fallback will reject them. Audit whether any test-fixture / real
  artifact uses the legacy shape before removing.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:32:37.493892Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T22:50:24.260230Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T22:50:24.260027Z","close_reason":"All 8 children closed (8n0rm.1-8); shipped: fcp-auth-schema crate with AuthClaims + canonical CBOR + schema_version + versioning ADR, fcp-crypto label re-exports + with_claims + auto-GRANTS synthesis, legacy OPERATIONS fallback removed, cross-crate conformance tests, schema inventory + typed-claims ADR + versioning ADR docs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm.1","title":"8n0rm.1 — Exhaustive capability-token claim-schema inventory","description":"# 8n0rm.1 — Exhaustive capability-token claim-schema inventory

## Goal
Produce a single markdown artifact enumerating every CBOR label, every
string convention, and every legacy/fallback path that `fcp-crypto`'s
`CapabilityTokenBuilder` emits AND that `fcp-core`'s `CapabilityVerifier`
interprets. Output lives at
`docs/architecture/adr/8n0rm-claim-schema-inventory.md`.

## Why this is first
The epic wants to replace convention with a typed schema. Before we can
design the typed schema (8n0rm.2), we need a written, audited ground
truth of what the current schema actually is. That ground truth also
becomes the input to the round-trip conformance test (8n0rm.7).

## How

### 1. Enumerate builder-side emissions

```bash
rg -n "fcp2_claims::[A-Z_]+|cwt_claims::[A-Z_]+" crates/fcp-crypto/src/
```

For each label hit in the builder:
- Label constant name (e.g. `fcp2_claims::OPERATIONS`)
- Label integer value (look up in the `fcp2_claims` or `cwt_claims` module)
- Builder method that emits it (e.g. `CapabilityTokenBuilder::operations`)
- Rust value type the builder accepts (e.g. `&[&str]`)
- CBOR shape emitted (e.g. `ciborium::Value::Array` of `Value::Text`)

### 2. Enumerate verifier-side reads

```bash
rg -n "fcp2_claims::[A-Z_]+|cwt_claims::[A-Z_]+" crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs
```

For each label hit in the verifier:
- Label constant
- Call site (function + line)
- Rust type the verifier expects to deserialize
- What happens if the claim is missing / wrong type

### 3. Identify legacy/fallback paths

Specifically look for:
- Branches that accept multiple shapes for the "same" logical field
- Comments mentioning legacy/fallback/migration
- The known `capability.rs:1597-1621` GRANTS-fallback-to-OPERATIONS

### 4. Identify asymmetries

Any label the builder emits but the verifier does NOT read (potential
dead surface). Any label the verifier reads but the builder does NOT
emit (potential silent-ignore).

## Deliverable format

`docs/architecture/adr/8n0rm-claim-schema-inventory.md` with a table:

| Label | Int | Builder emits (type → CBOR) | Verifier reads (CBOR → type) | Semantic role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `fcp2_claims::CAPABILITY_ID` | ? | `&str → Value::Text` | `Value::Text → str` via `get_capability_id` | Which capability |
| `fcp2_claims::OPERATIONS` | ? | `&[&str] → Array<Text>` | read at capability.rs:1601, LEGACY fallback | Operation whitelist — deprecated by GRANTS |
| `fcp2_claims::GRANTS` | ? | ? (verify builder side) | read at capability.rs:1579, canonical | Operation grant list |
| ... | | | | |

Followed by a "Legacy / fallback paths" section that explicitly
documents each multi-shape branch.

## Definition of done
- File exists at the expected path
- Every label referenced in either crate appears in the table
- Legacy fallbacks are explicitly called out
- Asymmetries (builder-only or verifier-only labels) are flagged
- Commit references `(br-8n0rm.1)`

## Notes
- No code changes in this bead — research only.
- If the inventory reveals a surprising coupling point (e.g. verifier
  reads a claim that builder never emits), do NOT try to fix it here —
  file a follow-up bead.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm.2","title":"8n0rm.2 — ADR: typed claims architecture (Option A shared module vs Option B separate crate)","description":"# 8n0rm.2 — ADR: typed claims architecture (decide A vs B)

## Goal
Pick between two architectures for the typed auth-claim schema and
commit the decision to `docs/architecture/adr/8n0rm-typed-claims.md`.

## Options

### Option A — `AuthClaims` lives in `fcp-core`
- Add `fcp_core::capability::claims::AuthClaims` struct.
- `AuthClaims` implements `serde::{Serialize, Deserialize}` with
  canonical CBOR as the wire format (matching today's label constants).
- `fcp-crypto` depends on `fcp-core` (already does, via some shared
  types) and accepts `&AuthClaims` into `CapabilityTokenBuilder::build`.
- `fcp-core::CapabilityVerifier` parses signed bytes → `AuthClaims`
  directly.

**Pros:** Simple. `fcp-core` is the natural home for security semantics.
No new crate.  **Cons:** If `fcp-crypto` does NOT currently depend on
`fcp-core`, adding that edge might pull in too much. Must verify.

### Option B — New `fcp-auth-schema` crate
- Minimal no-deps-beyond-serde crate.
- `fcp-auth-schema::AuthClaims` is the canonical type.
- Both `fcp-crypto` and `fcp-core` depend on `fcp-auth-schema`.

**Pros:** Clean dependency graph. Protects both crates from each
other's non-schema surface.  **Cons:** One more crate to maintain.
Slightly more ceremony at crate-creation time (Cargo.toml entry,
workspace member, etc.).

## Decision inputs

1. **Does `fcp-crypto` already depend on `fcp-core`?** Run
   `cargo tree -p fcp-crypto | grep fcp-core` or inspect
   `crates/fcp-crypto/Cargo.toml`. If yes, Option A is free. If no,
   adding the edge is a design decision.
2. **Would adding the edge create a cycle?** Run
   `cargo metadata --format-version 1 | jq '.packages[] | select(.name
   == "fcp-core") | .dependencies[].name' | grep fcp-crypto`. If
   `fcp-core` depends on `fcp-crypto`, Option A creates a cycle and
   Option B is mandatory.

## Expected decision
If no cycle exists, **Option A** is preferred for simplicity. Only
pick Option B if cycle-breaking requires it OR if we foresee a third
consumer (e.g. a future `fcp-capability-issuer` tool) that would
benefit from a lean schema-only crate.

## Deliverable
`docs/architecture/adr/8n0rm-typed-claims.md` with:
- Context (why this decision exists)
- Option A / B / comparison
- Decision (which we picked)
- Consequences (crate-graph changes, migration shape)
- Open questions (if any)

Standard ADR format — short sections, high signal.

## Definition of done
- ADR file committed
- Decision verdict unambiguous ("We choose Option X because Y")
- Commit references `(br-8n0rm.2)`
- Status: closed

## Notes
- This bead is decision-only. No code change.
- If the ADR surfaces a third option (e.g. folding `fcp-crypto` into
  `fcp-core`), discuss in the ADR; don't silently pick it.
- The ADR should explicitly acknowledge that `fcp-auth-schema` is
  naming-space-adjacent to potential future crates like
  `fcp-mesh-schema` or `fcp-audit-schema` — picking the name
  `fcp-auth-schema` implies we may grow a family of schema crates.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:46:04.362192Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T19:54:44.705937Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T19:54:44.705624Z","close_reason":"ADR committed; Option B chosen (new fcp-auth-schema crate due to fcp-core->fcp-crypto dep direction)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:46:04.362192Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-8n0rm.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:47:06.058518Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
